title: Quantum Error Correction Assisted Axion Search in CMOS Spin Qubit Arrays
abstract: Searches for axion and axionlike dark matter based on solid-state spin qubits are fundamentally limited by strong longitudinal dephasing, which rapidly suppresses the sensitivity gains offered by entanglement. Here we show that quantum error correction (QEC) can substantially enhance axion search sensitivity in realistic semiconductor spin qubit platforms by mitigating this dominant noise source. By integrating an optimally chosen repetition code QEC with logical GHZ block entanglement, we derive closed-form expressions for the quantum Fisher information that explicitly incorporate the finite coherence time of the axion field. Our analysis demonstrates that modest QEC cycle frequencies are sufficient to significantly reduce the effective dephasing rate, thereby restoring a broad parameter regime in which entanglement-enhanced sensing surpasses the standard quantum limit. Projecting these results onto CMOS-compatible device parameters, we find that QEC-protected entangled sensing can revive otherwise inaccessible quantum advantages, yielding up to order-of-magnitude improvements in sensitivity to the axion-electron coupling $g_{ae}$. These results establish a practical and theoretically controlled pathway for using QEC to improve qubit array searches for physics beyond the Standard Model.
summary: The paper proposes using quantum error correction to enhance the detection of axion dark matter using silicon spin qubit arrays. By using a repetition code to suppress local dephasing, the authors show that entanglement-enhanced sensing can be restored, significantly improving the sensitivity to axion-electron coupling.
main_problem: Searching for axion dark matter using semiconductor spin qubits is limited by strong longitudinal dephasing, which suppresses the sensitivity gains of entanglement.
main_result: Quantum error correction (QEC) using a repetition code can mitigate local dephasing, restoring entanglement-enhanced sensing and providing up to an order-of-magnitude improvement in sensitivity to axion-electron coupling.
method: The authors derive closed-form expressions for Quantum Fisher Information by integrating an optimal repetition code with logical GHZ block entanglement and optimizing the logical block size.
model_or_system: The system consists of CMOS-compatible silicon spin qubit arrays (quantum dots) interacting with a classical, spatially coherent axion field that acts as an effective oscillating pseudo-magnetic field.
key_observables: Axion-electron coupling (gae), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), and spin polarization sidebands.
important_parameters: Axion mass (10^-6 to 10^-3 eV), dephasing rates (local and correlated), QEC cycle frequency, and the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL).
main_assumptions: The axion field is treated as a classical, spatially coherent field with a wavelength much larger than the qubit array; the repetition code specifically targets local phase-flip errors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of a planar silicon quantum dot array with gates and repetition code blocks; Figure 2 shows the sensitivity improvement gain as a function of logical size; Figure 3 illustrates QEC benefit regimes; Figure 5 shows the scaling of sensitivity with the total number of physical qubits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dark matter search problem, defines the spin-qubit-axion interaction Hamiltonian, implements a QEC-protected GHZ protocol, analyzes the resulting Fisher information and sensitivity gains, and concludes with a scaling analysis and projection onto realistic CMOS hardware parameters.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum metrology, as it demonstrates how active error correction can be used to preserve quantum coherence for sensing tasks in the presence of non-Markovian and correlated noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A schematic planar silicon quantum dot array. In this specific design, we focus on a quantum dot array in the silicon layer. By applying a gate electric field via gate P1, electrons accumulate in silicon and are confined vertically against the silicon oxide (indicated at the location of the two- dimensional electron gas). The single quantum dot is formed using gates P2-P5. The gates P2 and P4 provide confinement in the x-direction, while gate P5 provides confinement in the y-direction. P3 is used as the top gate of the quantum dot, accumulating a single electron in the potential well beneath. Then the quantum dot array repeats the set-up for P2-P4. In the upper half of the figure,...

FIG. 2. Sensitivity improvement gain factor η(kL) for QEC- protected GHZ blocks of logical size k, evaluated for fu- ture possible semiconductor spin qubit dephasing param- eters. Curves correspond to repetition codes of distance nrep = 3 −13, with peaks marked for the high confidence dis- tances nrep = 9 −13. The maximal enhancement ηmax grows monotonically with code distance, while the optimal GHZ size k decreases, reflecting suppression of uncorrelated dephasing by QEC. For comparison, the unencoded GHZ (grey) achieves only a weak enhancement and rapidly becomes worse than SQL for large k, whereas QEC recovers a finite and robust en- tangled sensing window. Uncorrelated dephasing is...

FIG. 3. Regimes of QEC benefit for entanglement-based ax- ion sensing. The color scale shows the maximal sensitivity enhancement factor ηmax on a logarithmic scale as a func- tion of the uncorrected single-qubit dephasing γlocTseg and the effective logical dephasing γeffTseg. White solid contours indicate the optimal logical GHZ size k∗ L, while the dashed contour marks ηmax = 1. The plot delineates distinct pa- rameter regions corresponding to different balances between signal amplification and dephasing in QEC-protected entan- gled sensing.

FIG. 5. Scaling of the projected axion-coupling sensitiv- ity with the total number of signal-bearing physical qubits N. The SQL benchmark follows the expected N −1/2 behav- ior. The encoded curve uses complete QEC-protected logi- cal GHZ blocks with (nrep, kL) = (13, 14), so the first com- plete encoded block appears at Nblock = nrepkL = 182. For N < Nblock, the encoded architecture has no complete block and therefore coincides with the SQL baseline. For larger N, complete encoded blocks are instantiated discretely, while leftover physical qubits are treated as SQL resources, pro- ducing the visible staircase. At large N, the encoded sen- sitivity approaches the SQL scaling with a constant...

FIG. 4. Comparison of the logical dephasing and achievable metrological gain for realistic and optimistic control fidelities. Left: pg = pm = 10−4 (future possible error parameters of Tab. I). The logical dephasing γeffTseg is minimized at a small repetition distance ncoh rep ≈3, while the metrological gain ηmax saturates at ∼5 for nrep ≳10 due to the measurement in- duced logical error floor. Right: pg = pm = 10−5, represent- ing a next-generation regime. Both γeffTseg and ηmax improve, with ηmax reaching above 10, but the qualitative tradeoff and the location of the coherence-optimal code distance remain unchanged.
